en.extract:
The black-headed bulbul (Microtarsus melanocephalos) is a member of the bulbul family, Pycnonotidae. It is found in forests in south-eastern Asia.


Taxonomy
The black-headed bulbul was formally described in 1788 by the German naturalist Johann Friedrich Gmelin in his revised and expanded edition of Carl Linnaeus's Systema Naturae. He placed it with the shrikes in the genus Lanius and coined the binomial name Lanius melanocephalos. The specific epithet combines the Ancient Greek melas meaning "black" with -kephalos meaning "-headed". Gmelin based his account on the "black-headed shrike" that had been described and illustrated in 1781 by the English ornithologist John Latham in his multi-volume work A General Synopsis of Birds. Latham did not specify the origin of his specimen but Gmelin gave the locality as the Sandwich Islands. This was an error and the type locality was designated as Sumatra by the Harry Oberholser in 1912.
Earlier in his book Gmelin used the name Lanius melanocephalus for a variety of the red-backed shrike Lanius collurio. This has led to difficulties with the taxonomy. In 1917 Oberholser argued that under the code of nomenclature then adopted by the American Ornithologists' Union, Lanius melanocephalos was pre-occupied by Lanius melanocephalus and the correct name under the rules would be Turdus atriceps Temminck 1822. Oberholser's proposal was generally adopted and atriceps was used as the specific epithet. In 2018 it was pointed out that under the International Code of Zoological Nomenclature published in 1999 the two names are not homonyms (and if they were Lanius melanocephalos would have priority as it was applied to a species rather than a variety). Gmelin is again now recognised as the authority for this species. The black-headed bulbul was formerly placed in the genus Pycnonotus but when this genus was found to be polyphyletic  it was moved to the genus Brachypodius that had been introduced in 1845 by the English zoologist Edward Blyth. In 2025 in was moved a more broadly defined Microtarsus by the AviList team.
Until 2008, the Andaman bulbul was considered as a subspecies of the black-headed bulbul.
Four subspecies are recognized:

M. m. melanocephalos (Gmelin, JF, 1788) [synonym B. m. atriceps (Temminck, 1822)]: found in north-eastern India and Bangladesh though Southeast Asia to the Greater Sunda Islands and western Philippines
M. m. hyperemnus Oberholser, 1912: Found on western Sumatran islands
M. m. baweanus (Finsch, 1901): originally described as a separate species. Found on Bawean (north of Java)
M. m. hodiernus Bangs & Peters, JL, 1927: originally described as a separate species. Found on Maratua Island (off eastern Borneo)


Description

The black-headed bulbul is 16–18 cm (6.3–7.1 in) in overall length and weighs 20–30 g (0.71–1.06 oz). It has a mainly olive-yellow plumage with a glossy bluish-black head. A rare grey morph where most of the olive-yellow is replaced by grey also exists. It resembles the black-crested bulbul, but has blue eyes (though not reliable in juveniles), a broad yellow tip to the tail, and never shows a crest (however, some subspecies of the black-crested are also essentially crestless, but they have red or yellow throats).


Behavior and ecology
The black-headed bulbul feeds mainly on small fruit and berries, but will also take insects. It commonly occurs in small flocks of 6-8 individuals.


References

The '''black-headed bulbul''' ('''''Microtarsus melanocephalos''''') is a member of the [[bulbul]] family, Pycnonotidae. It is found in forests in south-eastern Asia.<ref name=iucn/>

==Taxonomy==
The black-headed bulbul was [[Species description|formally described]] in 1788 by the German naturalist [[Johann Friedrich Gmelin]] in his revised and expanded edition of [[Carl Linnaeus]]'s ''[[Systema Naturae]]''. He placed it with the shrikes in the [[genus]] ''[[Lanius]]'' and coined the [[binomial nomenclature|binomial name]] ''Lanius melanocephalos''.<ref name=gmelin>{{ cite book | last=Gmelin | first=Johann Friedrich | author-link=Johann Friedrich Gmelin| year=1788 | title=Systema naturae per regna tria naturae: secundum classes, ordines, genera, species, cum characteribus, differentiis, synonymis, locis | edition=13th | volume=1, Part 1 | language=Latin | location=Lipsiae [Leipzig] | publisher=Georg. Emanuel. Beer | page=309 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/2896909 }}</ref> The specific epithet combines the [[Ancient Greek]] ''melas'' meaning "black" with ''-kephalos'' meaning "-headed".<ref>{{cite book | last=Jobling | first=James A. | year=2010| title=The Helm Dictionary of Scientific Bird Names | publisher=Christopher Helm | location=London | isbn=978-1-4081-2501-4 | page=246 | url=https://archive.org/stream/Helm_Dictionary_of_Scientific_Bird_Names_by_James_A._Jobling#page/n246/mode/1up }}</ref> Gmelin based his account on the "black-headed shrike" that had been described and illustrated in 1781 by the English ornithologist [[John Latham (ornithologist)|John Latham]] in his multi-volume work ''A General Synopsis of Birds''.<ref name=latham>{{ cite book | last=Latham | first=John | author-link=John Latham (ornithologist) | year=1781 | title=A General Synopsis of Birds | volume=1, Part 1 | publisher=Printed for Leigh and Sotheby | location=London | page=165 No. 12, Plate 6 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/33727679 }}</ref> Latham did not specify the origin of his specimen but Gmelin gave the [[type locality (biology)|locality]] as the [[Sandwich Islands]].<ref name=latham/><ref name=gmelin/> This was an error and the type locality was designated as [[Sumatra]] by the [[Harry C. Oberholser|Harry Oberholser]] in 1912.<ref>{{ cite journal | last=Oberholser | first=Harry C. | author-link=Harry C. Oberholser | year=1912 | title=Description of one hundred and four new species and subspecies of birds from the Barussan Islands and Sumatra | journal=Smithsonian Miscellaneous Collections | volume=60 | issue=7 | pages=1-22 [10, Footnote 1] | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/8910440 }}</ref><ref>{{ cite book | editor1-last=Mayr | editor1-first=Ernst | editor1-link=Ernst Mayr | editor2-last=Greenway | editor2-first=James C. Jr | year=1960 | title=Check-List of Birds of the World | volume=9 | publisher=Museum of Comparative Zoology  | place=Cambridge, Massachusetts  | page=228 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/14480903 }}</ref>

Earlier in his book Gmelin used the name ''Lanius melanocephalus'' for a variety of the [[red-backed shrike]] ''Lanius collurio''.<ref>{{ cite book | last=Gmelin | first=Johann Friedrich | author-link=Johann Friedrich Gmelin| year=1788 | title=Systema naturae per regna tria naturae: secundum classes, ordines, genera, species, cum characteribus, differentiis, synonymis, locis | edition=13th | volume=1, Part 1 | language=Latin | location=Lipsiae [Leipzig] | publisher=Georg. Emanuel. Beer | page=301 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/2896901 }}</ref> This has led to difficulties with the taxonomy. In 1917 Oberholser argued that under the code of nomenclature then adopted by the [[American Ornithologists' Union]], ''Lanius melanocephalos'' was pre-occupied by ''Lanius melanocephalus'' and the correct name under the rules would be ''Turdus atriceps'' [[Coenraad Jacob Temminck|Temminck]] 1822.<ref>{{ cite journal | last=Oberholser | first=Harry C. | author-link=Harry C. Oberholser | year=1917 | title=The birds of Bawean Island, Java Sea | journal=Proceedings of the United States National Museum | volume=52 | issue=2175 | pages=183-198 [193, Footnote 4] | doi=10.5479/si.00963801.52-2175.183 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/7765443 }}</ref><ref>{{ cite book | date=1908 | title=The Code of Nomenclature adopted by the American Ornithologists' Union | edition=Revised | location=New York | publisher=American Ornithologists' Union | page=lvii, Canon XXX, (b) | url=https://babel.hathitrust.org/cgi/pt?id=hvd.32044062470786&view=1up&seq=64 }}</ref><ref>{{ cite book | last=Temminck | first=Coenraad Jacob  | author-link=Coenraad Jacob Temminck | year=1838 | orig-date=1822 | title=Nouveau recueil de planches coloriées d'oiseaux, pour servir de suite et de complément aux planches enluminées de Buffon | volume=2 | at=Plate 147 | publisher=F.G. Levrault | location=Paris | language=French | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/35246936 }} The 5 volumes were originally issued in 102 ''livraison'' (parts), 1820-1839.</ref> Oberholser's proposal was generally adopted and ''atriceps'' was used as the specific epithet. In 2018 it was pointed out that under the [[International Code of Zoological Nomenclature]] published in 1999 the two names are not homonyms (and if they were ''Lanius melanocephalos'' would have priority as it was applied to a species rather than a variety).<ref>{{ cite web | title=Chapter 6: Validity of names and nomenclatural acts. Article 24 | work=International Code Of Zoological Nomenclature | year=1999 |  publisher=International Commission on Zoological Nomenclature | edition=4th | url=https://www.iczn.org/the-code/the-code-online/?article=24&nfv=#1 }}</ref> Gmelin is again now recognised as the authority for this species. The black-headed bulbul was formerly placed in the genus ''[[Pycnonotus]]'' but when this genus was found to be [[polyphyletic]]  it was moved to the genus ''[[Brachypodius]]'' that had been introduced in 1845 by the English zoologist [[Edward Blyth]].<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Shakya|first1=Subir B. |last2=Sheldon|first2=Frederick H. | year=2017 |title=The phylogeny of the world's bulbuls (Pycnonotidae) inferred using a supermatrix approach | journal=Ibis |volume=159 |issue=3  |pages=498–509 |doi=10.1111/ibi.12464 | doi-access=free}}</ref><ref name=ioc>{{cite web| editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela Rasmussen | date=January 2023 | title=Bulbuls | work=IOC World Bird List Version 13.1 | url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/bulbuls/ | publisher=International Ornithologists' Union | access-date=18 May 2023}}</ref> In 2025 in was moved a more broadly defined ''[[Microtarsus]]'' by the [[AviList]] team.<ref name=avilist>{{ cite web | author=AviList Core Team | date=2025 | title=AviList: The Global Avian Checklist, v2025 | doi=10.2173/avilist.v2025 | doi-access=free | url=http://www.avilist.org/checklist/v2025/ | access-date=17 November 2025 }}</ref>

Until 2008, the [[Andaman bulbul]] was considered as a subspecies of the black-headed bulbul.<ref name=ioc/>

Four [[subspecies]] are recognized:<ref name=ioc/>

* ''M. m. melanocephalos'' <small>(Gmelin, JF, 1788)</small> [synonym ''B. m. atriceps'' <small>([[Coenraad Jacob Temminck|Temminck]], 1822)</small>]: found in north-eastern India and Bangladesh though Southeast Asia to the [[Greater Sunda Islands]] and western Philippines
* ''M. m. hyperemnus'' <small>[[Harry C. Oberholser|Oberholser]], 1912</small>: Found on western [[Sumatra]]n islands
* ''M. m. baweanus'' <small>([[Otto Finsch|Finsch]], 1901)</small>: originally described as a separate species. Found on [[Bawean]] (north of [[Java]])
* ''M. m. hodiernus'' <small>[[Outram Bangs|Bangs]] & [[James Lee Peters|Peters, JL]], 1927</small>: originally described as a separate species. Found on [[Derawan Islands|Maratua Island]] (off eastern [[Borneo]])

==Description==
[[file:Pycnonotus atriceps-20031005.jpg|thumb|left]]

The black-headed bulbul is {{cvt|16|-|18|cm}} in overall length and weighs {{cvt|20|-|30|g}}. It has a mainly olive-yellow [[plumage]] with a glossy bluish-black head. A rare grey [[Morph (zoology)|morph]] where most of the olive-yellow is replaced by grey also exists.<ref name=hbw>{{ cite book | last1=Fishpool | first1=L.D.C. |last2=Tobias | first2=J.A. | year=2005 | chapter=Family Pycnonotidae (Bulbuls) | editor1-last=del Hoyo | editor1-first=J. | editor2-last=Elliott | editor2-first=A. | editor3-last=Christie | editor3-first=D.A. | title=Handbook of the Birds of the World | volume=10: Cuckoo-shrikes to Thrushes | location=Barcelona, Spain | publisher=Lynx Edicions | isbn=978-84-87334-72-6 | pages=124–250 [173] | chapter-url=https://archive.org/details/handbookofbirdso0010unse/page/173/mode/1up | chapter-url-access=registration }}</ref> It resembles the [[black-crested bulbul]], but has blue eyes (though not reliable in juveniles), a broad yellow tip to the tail, and never shows a crest (however, some subspecies of the black-crested are also essentially crestless, but they have red or yellow throats).

==Behavior and ecology==

The black-headed bulbul feeds mainly on small fruit and berries, but will also take insects. It commonly occurs in small flocks of 6-8 individuals.
